sound_type: ultra-thin hum cloud -- high-pitched wingbeat whine, 400-600 Hz female, diffuse hum cloud, barely audible, threshold of perception, E=2, wetland habitat, first wing-produced sound in engine, QUADRANT II OPENS
ecosystem: Wetland. Standing water, marshes, swamps, wet meadows, pond edges. Aquatic larvae, aerial adults.
season: Spring through autumn. Peak swarming at dawn and dusk. Crepuscular and nocturnal.

coordinates.description: Reference for wetland habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Mosquito swarms near standing water, marshes, wet meadows.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY: Culicidae (41st). NEW ORDER: Diptera (true flies, mosquitoes, gnats). Fourth consecutive insect order in engine: Orthoptera (87-88), Hemiptera (89), Diptera (91). First wing-produced sound -- mechanical byproduct of flight, not a specialized sound organ. QUADRANT II OPENS.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Insecta. Returns after amphibian interlude at degree 90.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Culicidae from Latin culex ('gnat, mosquito'). Diptera from Greek di- ('two') + pteron ('wing'), referring to the single pair of functional wings.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple Culicidae genera (Aedes, Culex, Anopheles) contribute to the mosquito swarm sound in the PNW.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Diptera -- NEW ORDER. True flies. Fourth insect order in the engine. Distinguished by having two functional wings (the hind pair reduced to halteres).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- returns after amphibian interlude at degree 90 (spring peeper-like chorus, Hylidae).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Culicidae is the 41st family and the first Diptera family in the engine.

song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 450-550 Hz. Thin, reedy tone in the upper register.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song or call. WINGBEAT FREQUENCY. Mechanical byproduct of flight.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Continuous during flight. Individual mosquito: hours of activity. Swarm: hours at dawn/dusk.
song_metrics.note_structure: No note structure. Continuous tone at wingbeat frequency. Thin and unmodulated.
song_metrics.note_types: Wingbeat tone only. Males may modulate frequency during courtship 'duets' with females.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous hum. Swarm: hundreds of overlapping tones create a diffuse hum cloud.
song_metrics.timbre: Ultra-thin. Reedy. High-pitched. Whining. Barely audible individually. Diffuse as a swarm.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=2. Barely audible beyond a few meters for individuals. Swarm: audible as a diffuse hum cloud but still at the threshold of perception. The quietest sound in the species engine.
song_metrics.tempo: Wingbeat: 300-600 beats per second (female), 600-800 (male). Continuous. Not rhythmic in any perceivable pattern.
song_metrics.context: MATING SWARMS: males form hovering columns near water at dawn/dusk. Females enter swarms. Wingbeat frequency matching is part of mate recognition. The sound is a byproduct of flight that has been co-opted for communication.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Culicidae genera in PNW wetlands. All produce similar wingbeat frequencies within the family range.
song_metrics.key_property: ULTRA-THIN HUM CLOUD -- the 92nd acoustic element. First wing-produced sound. Mechanical byproduct of flight. 400-600 Hz. Barely audible. Diffuse hum cloud at threshold of perception. E=2. NEW FAMILY Culicidae (41st). NEW ORDER Diptera. QUADRANT II OPENS.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Small, slender flies with long legs, narrow wings, elongated proboscis. Body length 3-6 mm typically. Fragile appearance.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 91: (1) LONG PROBOSCIS -- elongated mouthpart for feeding. (2) NARROW WINGS with scales along veins -- diagnostic for Culicidae. (3) LONG, SLENDER LEGS. (4) SMALL SIZE -- 3-6 mm body. (5) CHARACTERISTIC WHINING FLIGHT SOUND.
physical_description.skin_detail: Exoskeleton. Covered in fine scales and setae. Body coloration varies by genus -- Aedes often black with white markings, Culex brownish.
physical_description.field_id: Detected primarily by SOUND -- the high-pitched whine near the ear. Also by tactile sensation (landing on skin). Swarms visible as hovering columns of tiny insects near water at dawn/dusk.

nodes.title: QUADRANT II OPENS -- Degree 91
nodes.description: First degree of Quadrant II on species side. Opens with a whisper -- E=2. The quietest sound yet. Thirty-first consecutive original CSV.

nodes.title: Ultra-Thin Hum Cloud -- 92nd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: High-pitched wingbeat whine. 400-600 Hz. Diffuse hum cloud. Barely audible. Threshold of perception. E=2. First wing-produced sound.

nodes.title: NEW ORDER: Diptera (Fourth Insect Order)
nodes.description: Diptera = true flies, mosquitoes. Fourth insect order: Orthoptera, Hemiptera, Diptera. NEW FAMILY: Culicidae (41st).

nodes.title: First Wing-Produced Sound
nodes.description: New sound category. Not voice, stridulation, or tymbal. Pure wing vibration -- mechanical byproduct of flight, not communication.

nodes.title: Wetland Habitat
nodes.description: Standing water, marshes, swamps. Aquatic larvae, aerial adults. Connects to muskrat marsh (85) and peeper wet meadow (90).

nodes.title: The Hovercraft Parallel
nodes.description: Both E=2. Hovercraft: sustained crush, maximum volume, minimum audience. Mosquito: ultra-thin hum, minimum volume, maximum ubiquity. Both at the threshold.

cross_references.relationship: INSECT CONTINUES. Cicada (Hemiptera, tymbal) to mosquito (Diptera, wing vibration). Different orders, different sound mechanisms. The insect diversity of the engine expands.
INSECT QUARTET. Cricket (Orthoptera, stridulation), katydid (Orthoptera), cicada (Hemiptera, tymbal), mosquito (Diptera, wing). Four insects, three orders, three sound mechanisms.
INSECT DIVERSITY. Katydid (Orthoptera, stridulation) and mosquito (Diptera, wing vibration). Different orders, different mechanisms. The insect streak extends.
WETLAND SHARED. Pacific Treefrog in wet habitats (86). Mosquito in wetlands (91). Both associated with standing water. Both crepuscular/nocturnal.
WETLAND NEIGHBOR. Muskrat in marshes (85). Mosquito in wetlands (91). Overlapping habitats. Both depend on standing water.
